source_paper_title: Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Jiang_2025, title={Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Jiang, Jiashuo and Ma, Will and Zhang, Jiawei}, year={2025}, month=apr }
context:
Page 6, Introduction, item 2 in the list of new results.

It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of \(k\)-unit IID prophet inequality.
title: Prove equality of limiting and worst-case IID k-unit prophet guarantees
background_latex:
Consider the multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid constraint of rank \(k\). There are \(n\) sequentially arriving agents \(i=1,\dots,n\). Each agent has a nonnegative valuation \(R_i\) revealed upon arrival, drawn independently from a known distribution.

In the IID setting, \(R_1,\dots,R_n\) are independent and identically distributed. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, and may accept at most \(k\) agents total.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value achieved by the optimal online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on an instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark: the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\).

For fixed \(k\ge 1\) and \(n>k\), define the tight (worst-case) competitive ratio in the IID setting as
\[
\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},
\]
where \(\mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) is the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.

Define the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit ratio (if it exists) as \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\), and define the overall worst-case-in-\(n\) ratio as \(\inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\) (with the implicit constraint \(n>k\)).
problem_definition_latex:
For each fixed integer \(k>1\), determine whether the following equality holds:
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} \,=\, \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},
\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)}\) is the tight competitive ratio of the optimal online policy against the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(n\) agents and \(k\) selection slots.
latex:
Consider the multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid constraint of rank \(k\). There are \(n\) sequentially arriving agents \(i=1,\dots,n\). Each agent has a nonnegative valuation \(R_i\) revealed upon arrival, drawn independently from a known distribution.

In the IID setting, \(R_1,\dots,R_n\) are independent and identically distributed. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, and may accept at most \(k\) agents total.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value achieved by the optimal online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on an instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark: the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\).

For fixed \(k\ge 1\) and \(n>k\), define the tight (worst-case) competitive ratio in the IID setting as
\[
\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},
\]
where \(\mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) is the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.

Define the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit ratio (if it exists) as \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\), and define the overall worst-case-in-\(n\) ratio as \(\inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\) (with the implicit constraint \(n>k\)).

For each fixed integer \(k>1\), determine whether the following equality holds:
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} \,=\, \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},
\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)}\) is the tight competitive ratio of the optimal online policy against the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(n\) agents and \(k\) selection slots.
location_in_paper: Page 6, Introduction, item 2 in the list of new results.
open_problem_quote: It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of \(k\)-unit IID prophet inequality.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement concerns an equality between an \(n\to\infty\) limit and an infimum over all \(n\), both defined via worst-case ratios over a large (infinite-dimensional) family of IID value distributions. While the paper provides a semi-infinite LP for fixed \((k,n)\), exhaustive search over distributions is not a finite combinatorial space, making automated counterexample hunting nontrivial.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: For fixed \((k,n)\), the paper gives an LP-based characterization that can be discretized and numerically evaluated, providing an automatic fitness signal for candidate policies or adversarial distributions. However, the open question is an analytic identity for all \(n\) and fixed \(k>1\), so an evolutionary loop would at best generate evidence or conjectured extremal instances rather than produce a proof.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
